Amantadine specifically targets dyskinesia but does not address wearing-off
explanation.pearls: Entacapone increases levodopa bioavailability by approximately 35% by inhibiting peripheral catechol-O-methyltransferase, effectively extending the half-life from approximately 1.5 hours to 2.5 hours; the levodopa dose may need to be reduced by 10-30% when entacapone is added to avoid worsening dyskinesia.

subtopic_detail: Submassive PE risk stratification and management
stem: A 54-year-old man presents with acute dyspnoea and pleuritic chest pain that began 4 hours ago. He had a left total knee replacement 12 days ago and has been on enoxaparin thromboprophylaxis but missed the past 2 doses. CTPA confirms bilateral segmental and subsegmental pulmonary emboli. His troponin is mildly elevated and an echocardiogram shows right ventricular dilatation with hypokinesis of the RV free wall but preserved apical contractility.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 108, BP 104/68, RR 24, SpO2 92% on 4 L/min nasal prongs
data_table.Examination: Elevated JVP, right parasternal heave, no peripheral oedema, left knee surgical wound healing well
data_table.Investigations: Troponin 124 ng/L (ULN 14), BNP 380 pg/mL, CTPA bilateral segmental PE, RV:LV ratio 1.3, echo McConnell sign positive, lactate 2.1 mmol/L

lead_in: What is the most appropriate management strategy?

options.text: Immediate systemic alteplase 100 mg IV over 2 hours for massive PE protocol
options.rationale: Systemic thrombolysis is indicated for massive PE with haemodynamic instability (sustained SBP <90 mmHg). This patient has submassive PE with RV dysfunction but is haemodynamically stable (SBP 104). Thrombolysis in this population carries significant bleeding risk, particularly 12 days post-surgery, without proven mortality benefit. The PEITHO trial showed reduced haemodynamic decompensation but increased major bleeding and stroke with thrombolysis in submassive PE. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Haematology; PEITHO trial)

options.text: Commence oral rivaroxaban 15 mg twice daily and discharge with outpatient review
options.rationale: This patient has submassive PE with RV dysfunction and elevated biomarkers indicating intermediate-high risk. Outpatient management is only appropriate for low-risk PE (sPESI 0, no RV dysfunction, normal biomarkers). This patient requires inpatient monitoring with close observation for haemodynamic deterioration that would trigger escalation to thrombolysis. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Haematology; ESC PE Guidelines)

options.text: Commence therapeutic dose IV heparin and monitor closely for haemodynamic deterioration
options.rationale: Submassive PE (intermediate-high risk: RV dysfunction plus elevated biomarkers) is managed with therapeutic anticoagulation and close haemodynamic monitoring, with thrombolysis reserved for clinical deterioration. IV unfractionated heparin is preferred over LMWH in this setting because it is titratable and rapidly reversible if thrombolysis or intervention becomes necessary. The recent surgery also favours UFH for its reversibility. Serial assessment for haemodynamic deterioration is essential. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Haematology; ESC PE Guidelines)

options.text: Refer for urgent catheter-directed thrombolysis as a lower-bleeding-risk approach
options.rationale: Catheter-directed therapy is an emerging option for submassive PE but is not the standard first-line approach. It requires specialist interventional radiology availability and is typically considered when systemic thrombolysis is contraindicated or as rescue therapy. Current guidelines still recommend therapeutic anticoagulation with watchful waiting as the primary approach. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Haematology)

options.text: Insert an IVC filter and commence therapeutic anticoagulation concurrently
options.rationale: IVC filters are indicated when anticoagulation is absolutely contraindicated or when PE recurs despite adequate anticoagulation. This patient can receive anticoagulation. Prophylactic IVC filter insertion in addition to anticoagulation has not shown benefit in reducing PE-related mortality and adds procedural risks and long-term filter complications. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Haematology; PREPIC2 trial)

explanation.summary: Submassive (intermediate-risk) PE is characterised by RV dysfunction on imaging or echo with elevated cardiac biomarkers but preserved systemic blood pressure. Management is therapeutic anticoagulation with close monitoring. Systemic thrombolysis is reserved for haemodynamic deterioration. The PEITHO trial showed that tenecteplase reduced haemodynamic decompensation in submassive PE but increased major bleeding and stroke, supporting a watchful waiting approach with rescue thrombolysis if needed.
key_points: Submassive PE = RV dysfunction plus elevated biomarkers with stable haemodynamics
IV UFH is preferred for its titrability if escalation to thrombolysis may be needed
McConnell sign (RV free wall hypokinesis with apical sparing) is specific for acute RV strain
Thrombolysis is reserved for haemodynamic deterioration in submassive PE
explanation.pearls: The McConnell sign (RV free wall hypokinesis with preserved apical contractility) has a specificity of approximately 94% for acute PE-related RV strain and distinguishes it from chronic RV dysfunction, where global hypokinesis is more typical.

stem: A 69-year-old woman is being treated in hospital for an acute PE with IV unfractionated heparin. On day 8 of heparin therapy, routine bloods show her platelet count has fallen from 210 x10^9/L on admission to 78 x10^9/L. She has developed new left calf swelling and a compression ultrasound confirms a new left popliteal DVT. She denies bleeding symptoms. Her 4Ts score is calculated at 7 points.

data_table.Examination: Left calf swollen and tender, no skin necrosis at heparin injection sites, no petechiae, no evidence of bleeding
data_table.Investigations: Platelets 78 x10^9/L (baseline 210), Hb 124 g/L stable, INR 1.0, APTT 68 seconds (on heparin), fibrinogen 4.2 g/L, 4Ts score 7 (high probability)

lead_in: What is the most appropriate immediate management?

options.text: Cease heparin and commence low molecular weight heparin as an alternative
options.rationale: LMWH has significant cross-reactivity with heparin-induced antibodies (approximately 90%) and is absolutely contraindicated in HIT. Switching from UFH to LMWH does not resolve the pathological process and may worsen thrombosis. A non-heparin anticoagulant must be used. (Source: HSANZ; Therapeutic Guidelines: Haematology)

options.text: Cease heparin and transfuse platelets to maintain a count above 100 x10^9/L
options.rationale: Platelet transfusion in HIT is generally avoided as it may fuel further antibody-mediated platelet activation and thrombosis. The thrombocytopenia in HIT is consumptive due to platelet activation, not due to production failure. Platelet transfusion is reserved for life-threatening bleeding or critical procedures. (Source: HSANZ; Therapeutic Guidelines: Haematology)

options.text: Cease heparin and commence warfarin 5 mg daily targeting INR 2.0-3.0
options.rationale: Warfarin must not be initiated during acute HIT before platelet recovery. Warfarin causes a transient drop in protein C, creating a prothrombotic state that can precipitate venous limb gangrene and skin necrosis in the setting of HIT. Warfarin may be introduced once platelets have recovered above 150 x10^9/L on a non-heparin anticoagulant. (Source: HSANZ; Therapeutic Guidelines: Haematology)

options.text: Continue heparin at the current dose until HIT antibody results are confirmed
options.rationale: With a 4Ts score of 7 (high probability) and new thrombosis, the clinical probability of HIT is very high. Continuing heparin while awaiting confirmatory testing risks further life-threatening thrombotic complications. Heparin must be ceased immediately and a non-heparin anticoagulant started empirically when the clinical suspicion is high. (Source: HSANZ; Therapeutic Guidelines: Haematology)

options.text: Cease all heparin and commence IV argatroban infusion with monitoring
options.rationale: Argatroban is a direct thrombin inhibitor that does not cross-react with HIT antibodies. In suspected HIT with high clinical probability (4Ts score >=6) and active thrombosis, all heparin must be ceased immediately and a non-heparin anticoagulant commenced. Argatroban is monitored via APTT and is the preferred agent in Australia for HIT with hepatic dosing adjustment. Danaparoid is an alternative. Confirmatory anti-PF4 antibody testing should be sent but must not delay treatment. (Source: HSANZ; Therapeutic Guidelines: Haematology)

explanation.summary: HIT is an immune-mediated prothrombotic disorder caused by antibodies against the heparin-PF4 complex. It typically occurs 5-10 days after heparin initiation (or sooner with prior exposure). Despite the thrombocytopenia, the primary risk is thrombosis, not bleeding. Management requires immediate heparin cessation and initiation of a non-heparin anticoagulant. Warfarin must not be started until platelets recover. The 4Ts score guides clinical probability assessment.
key_points: HIT is a prothrombotic condition despite thrombocytopenia
Cease all heparin and commence a non-heparin anticoagulant immediately
LMWH is contraindicated due to >90% cross-reactivity with HIT antibodies
Do not start warfarin until platelets recover above 150 x10^9/L
explanation.pearls: Argatroban is hepatically cleared (preferred in renal impairment) with a half-life of 45 minutes, making it highly titratable; it artificially elevates the INR, so overlap to warfarin requires monitoring with a chromogenic factor X assay rather than INR alone.

subtopic_detail: Type A aortic dissection management decision
stem: A 59-year-old man with a history of poorly controlled hypertension and a known bicuspid aortic valve presents to the emergency department with acute severe central chest pain radiating to his back. He is diaphoretic and anxious. A CT angiogram confirms a Stanford Type A aortic dissection with an intimal flap originating 2 cm above the aortic root and extending into the descending aorta. The aortic root measures 5.8 cm. There is a moderate pericardial effusion but no evidence of cardiac tamponade.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 102, BP 172/96 right arm, 148/84 left arm (>20 mmHg discrepancy), RR 24, SpO2 97%
data_table.Examination: New early diastolic murmur at left sternal edge, reduced left radial pulse compared with right, no focal neurological deficits
data_table.Investigations: CTA: Type A dissection from aortic root to descending aorta, aortic root 5.8 cm, moderate pericardial effusion, troponin 48 ng/L, creatinine 96 micromol/L

lead_in: What is the most appropriate definitive management?

options.text: Emergency cardiothoracic surgical repair with aortic root replacement
options.rationale: Stanford Type A aortic dissection is a surgical emergency with approximately 1-2% mortality per hour if untreated. This patient has multiple indications for urgent surgery: Type A dissection, aortic root involvement with dilatation (5.8 cm), new aortic regurgitation (early diastolic murmur), pericardial effusion suggesting haemopericardium risk, and a bicuspid aortic valve. Anti-impulse therapy should be commenced while arranging emergent surgery. (Source: CSANZ; Therapeutic Guidelines: Cardiovascular; BMJ Best Practice: Aortic Dissection)

options.text: Conservative medical management with IV esmolol targeting HR below 60 and SBP below 120
options.rationale: Medical management alone is appropriate for uncomplicated Type B dissection but carries unacceptable mortality in Type A dissection (approximately 50% at 2 weeks). The involvement of the ascending aorta poses immediate risk of aortic rupture, tamponade, coronary malperfusion, and acute severe aortic regurgitation. Anti-impulse therapy is essential while awaiting surgery but is not definitive management. (Source: CSANZ; BMJ Best Practice: Aortic Dissection)

options.text: Endovascular stent-graft repair of the descending aortic component only
options.rationale: Endovascular repair (TEVAR) is not appropriate for the ascending aorta due to the proximity of the coronary ostia, aortic valve, and arch vessels. Type A dissections involving the ascending aorta require open surgical repair. TEVAR is used for complicated Type B dissections involving the descending aorta. (Source: CSANZ; BMJ Best Practice: Aortic Dissection)

options.text: Urgent pericardiocentesis followed by reassessment for surgical candidacy
options.rationale: Pericardiocentesis in aortic dissection with haemopericardium is dangerous as it may remove the tamponade effect that is limiting further aortic haemorrhage. Rapid decompression can precipitate fatal exsanguination. Pericardiocentesis is only performed in extremis for imminent cardiac arrest. The pericardial effusion should be managed in the operating theatre during definitive surgical repair. (Source: CSANZ; BMJ Best Practice: Aortic Dissection)

options.text: Transfer to a cardiac catheterisation laboratory for coronary angiography first
options.rationale: Coronary angiography is not required before emergency Type A dissection surgery and delays definitive treatment. The mild troponin elevation may reflect coronary ostial involvement or demand ischaemia. Coronary assessment is performed intraoperatively. The delay associated with catheterisation is unacceptable given the 1-2% per hour mortality rate. (Source: CSANZ; Therapeutic Guidelines: Cardiovascular)

explanation.summary: Type A aortic dissection involving the ascending aorta is a surgical emergency. Without surgery, mortality is approximately 1-2% per hour in the first 48 hours. Medical anti-impulse therapy is commenced immediately but is a bridge to definitive surgical repair. Pericardiocentesis should be avoided unless cardiac arrest is imminent, as removing tamponade may precipitate fatal haemorrhage. Bicuspid aortic valve is a significant risk factor for aortic dissection due to associated aortopathy.
key_points: Type A dissection is a surgical emergency with 1-2% mortality per hour untreated
TEVAR is not suitable for ascending aortic pathology
Pericardiocentesis in dissection-related haemopericardium risks fatal decompression
Bicuspid aortic valve is associated with ascending aortopathy and dissection risk
explanation.pearls: The blood pressure differential >20 mmHg between arms in acute aortic dissection indicates differential subclavian artery involvement by the dissection flap, and the arm with the higher pressure reflects the true aortic pressure for titrating anti-impulse therapy.

stem: A 72-year-old woman has been receiving denosumab 60 mg subcutaneously every 6 months for 4 years for severe osteoporosis. Her T-scores have improved from -3.4 to -2.1 at the lumbar spine. She now wishes to cease denosumab due to recurrent injection site reactions. She has no history of fragility fractures while on treatment. Her renal function is preserved with an eGFR of 68 mL/min. She takes calcium and vitamin D supplementation.

data_table.Examination: No kyphosis, no tenderness on spinal percussion, BMI 24
data_table.Investigations: DXA T-score lumbar spine -2.1, femoral neck -1.8, eGFR 68 mL/min, corrected calcium 2.34 mmol/L, vitamin D 78 nmol/L

lead_in: What is the most appropriate approach to discontinuing denosumab?

options.text: Cease denosumab and arrange repeat DXA in 12 months to reassess bone density
options.rationale: Abrupt cessation of denosumab without bisphosphonate consolidation causes rapid rebound bone loss within 12-18 months, with bone mineral density returning to or below pre-treatment levels. This rebound is associated with a significantly increased risk of multiple vertebral fractures. Simply monitoring with DXA is insufficient to prevent this serious complication. (Source: Osteoporosis Australia; AMH)

options.text: Transition to oral alendronate 70 mg weekly starting 6 months after the last denosumab dose
options.rationale: Bisphosphonate consolidation therapy is recommended when discontinuing denosumab to prevent rebound bone loss and vertebral fractures. Oral alendronate should be commenced approximately 6 months after the last denosumab injection (when the next dose would have been due) to maintain anti-resorptive effect during the transition. A minimum of 12 months of bisphosphonate therapy is recommended. This patient's eGFR of 68 permits oral bisphosphonate use. (Source: Osteoporosis Australia; AMH; RACGP)

options.text: Halve the denosumab dose to 30 mg every 6 months as a gradual taper strategy
options.rationale: Dose reduction of denosumab is not an established tapering strategy. Half-dose denosumab does not provide adequate anti-resorptive effect, and there is no evidence that gradual dose reduction prevents the rebound phenomenon. The established approach is sequential bisphosphonate consolidation therapy after the last full dose. (Source: AMH; Osteoporosis Australia)

options.text: Cease denosumab and commence raloxifene 60 mg daily as alternative anti-resorptive
options.rationale: Raloxifene does not have sufficient anti-resorptive potency to prevent the rebound bone loss after denosumab cessation. Bisphosphonates are the only agents with evidence for preventing the post-denosumab rebound phenomenon due to their ability to bind to the bone matrix and provide sustained anti-resorptive effect. (Source: Osteoporosis Australia; AMH)

options.text: Continue denosumab indefinitely as discontinuation is contraindicated at any point
options.rationale: While long-term denosumab use is safe for up to 10 years based on extension studies, discontinuation is not absolutely contraindicated. It requires careful planning with bisphosphonate consolidation therapy. The patient's preference and injection site reactions are valid reasons to transition to an alternative, provided the switch is managed appropriately. (Source: Osteoporosis Australia; RACGP)

explanation.summary: Denosumab cessation triggers rapid rebound bone resorption mediated by a surge in osteoclast activity, with bone density returning to pre-treatment levels within 12-18 months and a significantly increased risk of multiple vertebral fractures. Bisphosphonate consolidation therapy is essential to mitigate this rebound. Oral alendronate commenced at the time of the next scheduled denosumab dose is the standard approach. Monitoring with bone turnover markers at 3-6 months can confirm adequate suppression.
key_points: Denosumab cessation causes rebound bone loss and vertebral fracture risk
Bisphosphonate consolidation is mandatory when stopping denosumab
Alendronate should commence at the time the next denosumab dose would be due
A minimum of 12 months of bisphosphonate therapy after denosumab is recommended
explanation.pearls: The rebound vertebral fracture risk after denosumab cessation peaks at 7-12 months post-last-dose, with an incidence of multiple vertebral fractures of approximately 5-10% in patients who cease without bisphosphonate consolidation, compared with less than 1% in those transitioned appropriately.

subtopic_detail: Tension pneumothorax vs massive haemothorax discrimination
stem: A 34-year-old man is brought to the emergency department by ambulance following a high-speed motorcycle collision. He is intubated and ventilated by paramedics due to progressive respiratory distress. He has an obvious right-sided flail chest segment. His ventilation pressures have been rising and his oxygen saturations are falling despite increasing FiO2. The right hemithorax is hyper-resonant to percussion with absent breath sounds. His trachea is deviated to the left. His neck veins are distended.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 132, BP 78/50, SpO2 82% on FiO2 1.0 via ventilator, peak airway pressure 42 cmH2O
data_table.Examination: Intubated, tracheal deviation to left, distended neck veins, absent breath sounds right, hyper-resonant right chest, right-sided flail segment, abdomen soft
data_table.Investigations: Nil yet available - point-of-care blood gas being processed

lead_in: What is the most appropriate immediate intervention?

options.text: Obtain a portable CXR before any intervention to confirm the suspected diagnosis
options.rationale: Tension pneumothorax is a clinical diagnosis requiring immediate intervention. Delaying treatment for radiological confirmation in a haemodynamically compromised patient risks cardiac arrest from obstructive shock. Imaging should never delay life-saving decompression when the clinical picture is clear. (Source: ATLS; ANZCOR)

options.text: Perform bilateral finger thoracostomies as part of a standard trauma approach
options.rationale: While bilateral finger thoracostomies are part of some rapid sequence approaches in intubated trauma patients, the clinical signs clearly lateralise to the right hemithorax. The left chest has no clinical indication for decompression. Unnecessary bilateral thoracostomies increase procedural risk and delay definitive management of the identified pathology. (Source: ATLS)

options.text: Administer a rapid 1 litre crystalloid bolus to address the obstructive shock first
options.rationale: While fluid resuscitation may provide transient haemodynamic support, the primary pathology is obstructive shock from tension pneumothorax. The mediastinal shift and impaired venous return will not resolve with fluid alone. Decompression of the tension pneumothorax must be performed immediately to restore venous return and cardiac output. (Source: ATLS; ANZCOR)

options.text: Perform immediate right-sided finger thoracostomy followed by intercostal drain insertion
options.rationale: This patient has classic signs of right-sided tension pneumothorax: hyper-resonance, absent breath sounds, tracheal deviation away from the affected side, distended neck veins (impaired venous return), hypotension, and rising ventilation pressures. In an intubated patient, finger thoracostomy (open decompression) in the 4th-5th intercostal space mid-axillary line is preferred over needle decompression due to higher success rate. An intercostal drain should be placed immediately after decompression. (Source: ATLS; ANZCOR)

options.text: Perform right-sided needle decompression at the 2nd intercostal space mid-clavicular line
options.rationale: Needle decompression at the 2nd intercostal space mid-clavicular line is the traditional pre-hospital technique for tension pneumothorax. However, in the ED with an already intubated patient, finger thoracostomy is preferred because needle decompression has a failure rate of up to 40% due to insufficient needle length or catheter kinking, particularly in patients with increased chest wall thickness. (Source: ATLS; ANZCOR)

explanation.summary: Tension pneumothorax in a ventilated trauma patient requires immediate decompression. The distinguishing clinical features from massive haemothorax are hyper-resonance (vs dullness) and distended neck veins (vs flat veins from hypovolaemia). In the ED with an intubated patient, finger thoracostomy is preferred over needle decompression due to higher success rates. The definitive treatment is intercostal drain insertion immediately after decompression.
key_points: Tension pneumothorax is a clinical diagnosis requiring immediate decompression
Hyper-resonance plus distended neck veins distinguishes tension pneumothorax from massive haemothorax
Finger thoracostomy is preferred over needle decompression in the ED setting
Needle decompression has up to 40% failure rate due to chest wall thickness
explanation.pearls: In trauma, massive haemothorax presents with ipsilateral dullness to percussion and flat neck veins (hypovolaemia), while tension pneumothorax presents with hyper-resonance and distended neck veins (obstructive shock); both cause absent breath sounds and tracheal deviation, making percussion and JVP the key discriminators.

stem: A 64-year-old woman with metastatic breast cancer is reviewed for management of a recurrent right-sided pleural effusion. She has required three therapeutic thoracenteses in the past 6 weeks, each draining approximately 1.5 litres with symptomatic relief lasting only 7-10 days. Pleural fluid cytology has confirmed malignant cells. She has an estimated life expectancy of 4-6 months and her performance status is ECOG 2. She is able to manage activities of daily living with assistance. She wishes to minimise hospital visits.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 88, BP 118/72, RR 20, SpO2 94% on room air
data_table.Examination: Stony dull percussion right base to mid-zone, reduced breath sounds, no contralateral effusion, mild cachexia
data_table.Investigations: CXR: large right pleural effusion, repeat thoracentesis yields 1.6 L straw-coloured exudative fluid, cytology positive for adenocarcinoma, CT shows no trapped lung

lead_in: What is the most appropriate management for her recurrent effusion?

options.text: Continue therapeutic thoracentesis as needed every 7-10 days in the clinic
options.rationale: Repeat thoracentesis every 7-10 days is burdensome for a patient who wishes to minimise hospital visits. It does not address the underlying recurrence and exposes the patient to repeated procedural risks including pain, infection, and pneumothorax. A definitive management strategy is preferred for rapidly recurring effusions. (Source: TSANZ; BMJ Best Practice: Malignant Pleural Effusion)
source_refs: BMJ Best Practice: Malignant Pleural Effusion

options.text: Insert a small-bore intercostal drain with talc slurry pleurodesis via the drain
options.rationale: Talc slurry pleurodesis via a chest drain can be effective but requires hospitalisation for several days until the drain output is minimal. For a patient prioritising reduced hospital visits, an indwelling pleural catheter allows outpatient drainage and may achieve spontaneous pleurodesis. The TIME2 trial showed equivalent symptom control between IPC and talc pleurodesis. (Source: TSANZ; TIME2 trial)

options.text: Insert an indwelling pleural catheter for outpatient intermittent drainage
options.rationale: An indwelling pleural catheter (IPC) is the preferred option for this patient. It allows outpatient drainage by the patient or a carer, minimising hospital visits. The TIME2 trial demonstrated equivalent breathlessness control between IPC and talc pleurodesis. IPCs also achieve spontaneous pleurodesis in approximately 45-50% of cases. For patients with limited life expectancy who prioritise staying out of hospital, IPC is the recommended approach per TSANZ guidelines. (Source: TSANZ; BMJ Best Practice: Malignant Pleural Effusion)
source_refs: BMJ Best Practice: Malignant Pleural Effusion

options.text: Refer for video-assisted thoracoscopic surgery with mechanical pleurodesis
options.rationale: VATS pleurodesis has the highest success rate for achieving pleurodesis but requires general anaesthesia and several days of hospitalisation. For a patient with ECOG 2, limited life expectancy, and a preference to minimise hospital visits, this is disproportionately invasive. It is more appropriate for patients with better performance status and longer expected survival. (Source: TSANZ; BMJ Best Practice: Malignant Pleural Effusion)
source_refs: BMJ Best Practice: Malignant Pleural Effusion

options.text: Commence systemic chemotherapy optimisation to reduce pleural fluid production
options.rationale: While systemic therapy may reduce effusion recurrence in chemosensitive tumours, it does not provide immediate symptomatic relief and its effect on pleural fluid is unpredictable. The recurrent effusion requires a mechanical drainage solution regardless of systemic therapy optimisation. (Source: BMJ Best Practice: Malignant Pleural Effusion)
source_refs: BMJ Best Practice: Malignant Pleural Effusion

explanation.summary: Recurrent malignant pleural effusion management depends on patient performance status, life expectancy, lung re-expansion capacity, and patient preference. Indwelling pleural catheters offer equivalent symptom control to talc pleurodesis with the advantage of outpatient management. The TIME2 trial is the landmark study comparing these approaches. Talc pleurodesis is preferred when the patient has good performance status and the lung fully re-expands. Trapped lung is a contraindication to pleurodesis as the visceral and parietal pleura cannot appose.
key_points: IPC is preferred for patients prioritising reduced hospital visits and limited life expectancy
TIME2 trial showed equivalent breathlessness control between IPC and talc pleurodesis
IPCs achieve spontaneous pleurodesis in approximately 45-50% of patients
Trapped lung (failure of lung re-expansion) is a contraindication to pleurodesis
explanation.pearls: The AMPLE-2 trial demonstrated that daily IPC drainage (vs symptom-guided drainage) increased spontaneous pleurodesis rates from 24% to 47% at 12 weeks, suggesting an aggressive early drainage strategy when aiming for catheter removal.

sources.label: BMJ Best Practice: Malignant Pleural Effusion

stem: A 56-year-old man with poorly controlled hypertension presents to the emergency department with sudden severe chest pain and dyspnoea. He has not taken his usual antihypertensives for the past 2 weeks. His ECG shows LVH with strain pattern and sinus tachycardia but no ST-elevation. A CXR shows bilateral pulmonary oedema with upper lobe diversion. His troponin is mildly elevated. An echocardiogram shows severely impaired LV systolic function (LVEF 25%) with no regional wall motion abnormalities. A CT aortogram excludes aortic dissection.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 118, BP 228/132, RR 32, SpO2 88% on 15 L/min non-rebreather mask
data_table.Examination: Diaphoretic, bilateral coarse crackles to mid-zones, elevated JVP, S3 gallop, no peripheral oedema, equal arm pressures
data_table.Investigations: ECG: sinus tachycardia, LVH with strain, troponin 68 ng/L, BNP 1840 pg/mL, creatinine 142 micromol/L, CXR bilateral pulmonary oedema, echo LVEF 25%

lead_in: What is the most appropriate initial pharmacological approach to blood pressure reduction?

options.text: IV labetalol 20 mg boluses targeting a 25% BP reduction over 6-8 hours
options.rationale: IV labetalol is a reasonable agent for many hypertensive emergencies but is relatively contraindicated in acute decompensated heart failure due to its beta-blocking effect, which may further depress cardiac output in a patient with LVEF 25%. Vasodilator therapy is preferred in hypertensive emergency complicated by acute pulmonary oedema. (Source: NHFA; Therapeutic Guidelines: Cardiovascular)

options.text: Oral amlodipine 10 mg and oral perindopril 5 mg as urgent oral stepdown therapy
options.rationale: Oral antihypertensives have slow and unpredictable onset in the acute setting. This patient has a hypertensive emergency with acute pulmonary oedema requiring rapid, titratable blood pressure reduction. IV therapy is mandatory. Oral agents may be introduced once the acute crisis is stabilised. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Cardiovascular; NHFA)

options.text: IV sodium nitroprusside infusion starting at 0.3 mcg/kg/min with arterial line monitoring
options.rationale: Sodium nitroprusside is effective but carries risks of cyanide and thiocyanate toxicity, reflex tachycardia, and coronary steal phenomenon. In the setting of acute heart failure with possible myocardial ischaemia (elevated troponin), GTN infusion is preferred as it reduces preload and afterload with a favourable coronary blood flow profile. Nitroprusside is reserved for refractory cases. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Cardiovascular; NHFA)

options.text: IV hydralazine 5 mg boluses every 20 minutes targeting SBP below 160 mmHg
options.rationale: Hydralazine causes direct arteriolar vasodilation with reflex tachycardia and increased cardiac output, which may worsen myocardial oxygen demand. It is poorly titratable due to unpredictable dose response and prolonged duration. In acute heart failure, agents that reduce preload are preferred to pure afterload reducers. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Cardiovascular)

options.text: IV GTN infusion starting at 10-20 mcg/min titrated to symptom relief and BP target
options.rationale: IV glyceryl trinitrate is the preferred agent for hypertensive emergency complicated by acute pulmonary oedema. It reduces both preload (venodilatation) and afterload at higher doses, providing rapid symptom relief. It has a favourable coronary vasodilatory profile relevant to this patient's elevated troponin. The target is a 25% MAP reduction in the first hour. Concurrent IV furosemide and non-invasive ventilation address the pulmonary oedema. (Source: NHFA; Therapeutic Guidelines: Cardiovascular)

explanation.summary: Hypertensive emergency management is organ-specific. In acute pulmonary oedema, IV GTN is preferred because it reduces preload and afterload while providing coronary vasodilatation. Beta-blockers are avoided in acute decompensated heart failure due to negative inotropic effects. The blood pressure target is a 25% MAP reduction in the first hour, not normalisation, to avoid organ hypoperfusion. Concurrent management with diuretics, oxygen, and consideration of non-invasive ventilation is essential.
key_points: IV GTN is first-line for hypertensive emergency with acute pulmonary oedema
Beta-blockers are relatively contraindicated in acute decompensated heart failure
Target 25% MAP reduction in the first hour to avoid organ hypoperfusion
IV furosemide and non-invasive ventilation are concurrent therapies
explanation.pearls: GTN at low doses (10-40 mcg/min) primarily causes venodilatation reducing preload, while at higher doses (>100 mcg/min) it additionally causes arteriolar dilatation reducing afterload; this dose-dependent pharmacology allows titration from pure preload reduction to combined preload-afterload reduction.

stem: A 24-year-old woman with bipolar I disorder has been stable on sodium valproate 1000 mg daily for 3 years with no manic or depressive episodes. She presents for routine review. She is currently single and not planning pregnancy. She uses condoms inconsistently as her only contraception. She was not counselled about the pregnancy prevention program when valproate was first prescribed.

data_table.Examination: Euthymic, no psychotic features, BMI 26, well-groomed, good rapport
data_table.Investigations: Valproate level 72 mg/L (target 50-100), LFTs normal, FBC normal, pregnancy test negative

lead_in: What is the most appropriate action regarding her valproate prescription?

options.text: Enrol in the pregnancy prevention program and ensure effective contraception before continuing
options.rationale: Australian TGA and ACSQHC requirements mandate that all women of childbearing potential on valproate must be enrolled in the valproate pregnancy prevention program, have an annual acknowledgement of risk form signed, and use highly effective contraception (LARC or combined methods). Condoms alone are insufficient. Valproate can be continued if the patient provides informed consent and uses effective contraception. Abrupt cessation risks destabilising her bipolar disorder. (Source: ACSQHC Valproate Safety; AMH)

options.text: Immediately cease valproate and switch to lithium as a safer alternative for her age
options.rationale: While lithium is preferred for bipolar disorder in women of childbearing age, abruptly ceasing valproate in a stable patient without establishing an alternative mood stabiliser risks precipitating mania or depression. If a switch is planned, it should be done gradually with overlapping treatment. The immediate priority is to address the missing pregnancy prevention program requirements. (Source: RANZCP Clinical Practice Guidelines; AMH)

options.text: Continue valproate unchanged as pregnancy is not currently planned by the patient
options.rationale: The pregnancy prevention program applies to ALL women of childbearing potential on valproate, regardless of current pregnancy plans. Unplanned pregnancies account for a significant proportion of pregnancies, and valproate exposure in early pregnancy causes neural tube defects in approximately 5-10% and neurodevelopmental effects in up to 40% of exposed children. Inconsistent condom use provides inadequate protection. (Source: ACSQHC Valproate Safety; AMH)

options.text: Add high-dose folic acid 5 mg daily to mitigate the teratogenic risk while continuing valproate
options.rationale: While folic acid supplementation is recommended for all women of childbearing potential, evidence does not support that it adequately mitigates valproate-specific teratogenicity. Valproate's teratogenic mechanism involves histone deacetylase inhibition and is not solely folate-mediated. Folic acid is an adjunct, not a substitute for effective contraception and the pregnancy prevention program. (Source: AMH; ACSQHC Valproate Safety)

options.text: Reduce valproate to the minimum effective dose of 500 mg daily to lower teratogenic risk
options.rationale: Dose reduction may lower the risk somewhat (teratogenicity is dose-dependent above 700-1000 mg/day) but does not eliminate it, and reducing from an effective dose risks destabilisation. The priority is enrollment in the pregnancy prevention program with effective contraception. Dose optimisation is a secondary consideration in the context of established efficacy at the current dose. (Source: AMH; RANZCP)

explanation.summary: Valproate carries the highest teratogenic risk of any commonly used mood stabiliser, causing neural tube defects in 5-10% and neurodevelopmental effects in up to 40% of exposed pregnancies. The Australian pregnancy prevention program mandates informed consent, effective contraception, and annual review for all women of childbearing potential. These requirements apply regardless of current pregnancy planning status due to the high rate of unplanned pregnancies.
key_points: Valproate pregnancy prevention program is mandatory for all women of childbearing potential
Highly effective contraception (LARC or combined methods) is required, not condoms alone
Annual risk acknowledgement form must be signed
Abrupt cessation of stable mood stabiliser risks relapse and should be avoided
explanation.pearls: Valproate exposure in the first trimester causes neural tube defects at a rate of approximately 5-10% (vs 0.1% background risk), with the critical window being weeks 3-4 post-conception, often before pregnancy is recognised - underlining why effective contraception must be established proactively.

stem: A 32-year-old woman is brought to the emergency department by ambulance after her housemate found her drowsy next to an empty blister pack of amitriptyline. The housemate estimates she may have taken up to 3 g approximately 90 minutes ago. She is drowsy but rousable. She has a history of depression and chronic back pain. An IV cannula has been placed and blood tests sent.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 128 (sinus tachycardia), BP 92/58, RR 16, SpO2 97% on room air, T 37.2, GCS 12 (E3 V4 M5)
data_table.Examination: Dilated pupils 7 mm bilaterally, dry flushed skin, urinary retention on bladder scan (450 mL), no clonus, mild generalised hyperreflexia
data_table.Investigations: ECG: sinus tachycardia 128, QRS duration 118 ms, right axis deviation, positive R wave in aVR 4 mm, QTc 488 ms

lead_in: What is the most appropriate immediate pharmacological intervention?

options.text: IV physostigmine 1 mg to reverse the anticholinergic toxidrome causing the tachycardia
options.rationale: Physostigmine is contraindicated in TCA overdose despite the anticholinergic features. It can precipitate seizures, bradycardia, and asystole by potentiating the cholinergic effects in the setting of sodium channel blockade. The anticholinergic features are managed supportively. (Source: Toxicology Guidelines; AMH)

options.text: IV sodium bicarbonate 1-2 mmol/kg bolus targeting a serum pH of 7.50-7.55
options.rationale: Sodium bicarbonate is the specific antidote for TCA-induced sodium channel blockade. The QRS duration of 118 ms (>100 ms) and positive R wave in aVR >3 mm indicate significant sodium channel blockade with risk of ventricular arrhythmias. Sodium bicarbonate works by alkalinisation (reducing drug-receptor binding) and providing a sodium load to overcome the channel blockade. Target serum pH 7.50-7.55 with repeated boluses. (Source: Toxicology Guidelines; Therapeutic Guidelines: Toxicology; Austin Health Toxicology)

options.text: Activated charcoal 50 g via nasogastric tube for gastrointestinal decontamination
options.rationale: Activated charcoal may be considered within 1-2 hours of TCA ingestion if the airway is protected. However, this patient has reduced GCS (12) with risk of further deterioration. The immediate priority is treating the sodium channel blockade with sodium bicarbonate, not gastrointestinal decontamination. Charcoal may be given concurrently if safe airway conditions permit. (Source: Toxicology Guidelines)

options.text: IV amiodarone 300 mg over 20 minutes to prevent ventricular tachyarrhythmia
options.rationale: Amiodarone and all Class IA and IC antiarrhythmics are contraindicated in TCA overdose. They worsen sodium channel blockade and prolong the QRS and QT intervals further, increasing arrhythmia risk. Sodium bicarbonate is the correct treatment for QRS widening in TCA toxicity. (Source: Toxicology Guidelines; AMH)

options.text: IV lipid emulsion 20% at 1.5 mL/kg bolus as rescue therapy for lipophilic toxicity
options.rationale: IV lipid emulsion may be considered in severe TCA toxicity refractory to sodium bicarbonate, particularly in cardiac arrest. However, it is not first-line therapy. Sodium bicarbonate is the established first-line treatment for TCA cardiotoxicity with QRS prolongation. Lipid emulsion is a rescue therapy for refractory cases. (Source: Toxicology Guidelines; Austin Health Toxicology)

explanation.summary: TCA overdose causes a distinctive toxidrome combining anticholinergic effects, cardiovascular toxicity (sodium channel blockade, alpha-blockade), and CNS depression. The ECG is the critical prognostic tool - QRS >100 ms and R wave in aVR >3 mm indicate significant cardiotoxicity with risk of seizures and ventricular arrhythmias. IV sodium bicarbonate is the specific treatment for sodium channel blockade, working through alkalinisation and sodium loading.
key_points: QRS >100 ms and R wave in aVR >3 mm indicate significant TCA cardiotoxicity
IV sodium bicarbonate is the specific antidote for TCA sodium channel blockade
Target serum pH 7.50-7.55 with repeated boluses
Class IA/IC antiarrhythmics and physostigmine are contraindicated
explanation.pearls: The R wave amplitude in aVR >3 mm has a sensitivity of 81% and specificity of 73% for predicting seizures in TCA overdose, and is a more reliable early marker of severe toxicity than the QRS duration alone.

subtopic_detail: Schizoaffective disorder vs bipolar I with psychosis
stem: A 29-year-old man is reviewed in the outpatient psychiatry clinic. Over the past 4 years, he has had three manic episodes with prominent grandiose and persecutory delusions and auditory hallucinations. Between these episodes, he has extended periods of 3-4 months where his mood is completely euthymic, yet he continues to experience persistent auditory hallucinations (commentary voices) and referential delusions. He is adherent with his medications. His mother has schizophrenia and his maternal uncle has bipolar I disorder.

data_table.Examination: Well-groomed, euthymic affect, engaged in conversation, describes ongoing auditory hallucinations present daily despite euthymic mood, referential ideation regarding television broadcasts, no thought disorder, insight partial
data_table.Investigations: Nil new - recent TFTs, FBC, metabolic panel all normal

lead_in: What is the most likely diagnosis?

options.text: Bipolar I disorder with psychotic features limited to mood episodes only
options.rationale: In bipolar I with psychotic features, psychotic symptoms occur exclusively during mood episodes (mania or depression) and resolve completely during euthymia. This patient has persistent hallucinations and delusions during extended euthymic periods, which is the key discriminating feature that points away from bipolar I with psychotic features. (Source: RANZCP; DSM-5-TR)

options.text: Schizophrenia with secondary mood episodes attributable to substance misuse
options.rationale: There is no mention of substance use, and the manic episodes are described as distinct episodic events consistent with a primary mood component. Schizophrenia does not include full manic episodes as part of its presentation. The co-occurrence of full affective episodes and persistent psychosis between them points to schizoaffective disorder. (Source: RANZCP; DSM-5-TR)

options.text: Delusional disorder with grandiose subtype and coincidental mood instability
options.rationale: Delusional disorder involves non-bizarre delusions without prominent hallucinations, and functioning is generally preserved apart from the delusional system. This patient has prominent auditory hallucinations (commentary voices) and full manic episodes, which exceed the criteria for delusional disorder. (Source: DSM-5-TR; RANZCP)

options.text: Schizoaffective disorder, bipolar type, with inter-episode psychotic symptoms
options.rationale: Schizoaffective disorder requires: (1) a major mood episode concurrent with Criterion A schizophrenia symptoms, (2) delusions or hallucinations for at least 2 weeks in the absence of a mood episode during the illness lifetime, and (3) mood symptoms present for the majority of the total illness duration. This patient meets all criteria: manic episodes with psychotic features, persistent psychosis during 3-4 month euthymic periods, and recurrent mood episodes comprising a substantial portion of his illness. (Source: RANZCP; DSM-5-TR; BMJ Best Practice: Schizoaffective Disorder)

options.text: Schizophrenia with comorbid cyclothymic disorder causing mood fluctuations
options.rationale: The manic episodes described are full manic episodes (with grandiosity and psychosis), not the hypomanic-depressive fluctuations of cyclothymia. Additionally, schizophrenia with a separate cyclothymia diagnosis does not account for the temporal relationship between psychotic and mood symptoms. The integrated diagnosis of schizoaffective disorder better captures this presentation. (Source: RANZCP; DSM-5-TR)

explanation.summary: Schizoaffective disorder occupies the diagnostic boundary between schizophrenia and mood disorders with psychotic features. The key discriminator from bipolar I with psychotic features is the presence of psychotic symptoms during extended euthymic periods (at least 2 weeks without mood symptoms). From schizophrenia, it is distinguished by the presence of prominent mood episodes comprising a substantial portion of the total illness. It is subtyped as bipolar or depressive type.
key_points: Psychosis persisting during euthymia distinguishes schizoaffective from bipolar with psychotic features
At least 2 weeks of psychosis without mood symptoms is required for schizoaffective diagnosis
Mood episodes must be present for the majority of the total illness duration
Family history often spans both schizophrenia and mood disorders spectrum
explanation.pearls: The DSM-5-TR criterion requiring delusions or hallucinations for >=2 weeks in the absence of a mood episode is the single most important discriminator; a careful longitudinal history documenting psychotic symptoms during euthymic intervals is more diagnostically valuable than any cross-sectional assessment.

stem: A 48-year-old man presents to his GP requesting help to quit smoking. He smokes 25 cigarettes per day and has smoked since age 16. He has tried nicotine patches twice in the past but relapsed within 2 weeks each time. He has a stable history of mild depression managed with sertraline 50 mg daily for 3 years. He has no history of psychosis, bipolar disorder, or seizures. He is motivated to quit and has set a target quit date in 2 weeks.

data_table.Examination: BMI 27, no acute distress, PHQ-9 score 4 (minimal depression)
data_table.Investigations: Nil recent required

lead_in: What is the most appropriate smoking cessation pharmacotherapy?

options.text: Nicotine patch 21 mg daily alone with a structured 12-week tapering plan
options.rationale: This patient has already failed two attempts with nicotine patches as monotherapy. Repeating the same intervention is unlikely to succeed. Varenicline has superior efficacy to NRT monotherapy in clinical trials and is appropriate after NRT failure. (Source: RACGP Smoking Cessation Guidelines; Therapeutic Guidelines: Addiction)

options.text: Bupropion sustained-release 150 mg daily for 3 days then 150 mg twice daily
options.rationale: Bupropion is a reasonable option for smoking cessation but has lower efficacy than varenicline in head-to-head trials (EAGLES study). Given two prior NRT failures, the most effective available agent should be offered. Bupropion would be appropriate if varenicline were contraindicated. (Source: RACGP Smoking Cessation Guidelines; EAGLES trial)

options.text: Varenicline 0.5 mg daily for 3 days, then 0.5 mg twice daily for 4 days, then 1 mg twice daily
options.rationale: Varenicline is the most effective single-agent smoking cessation pharmacotherapy, with quit rates approximately 2-3 times placebo. The standard titration (0.5 mg daily for days 1-3, 0.5 mg twice daily for days 4-7, then 1 mg twice daily from day 8) is started 1-2 weeks before the target quit date. The EAGLES trial confirmed no increased neuropsychiatric risk in patients with stable psychiatric conditions. Sertraline interaction is minimal. (Source: RACGP Smoking Cessation Guidelines; Therapeutic Guidelines: Addiction; EAGLES trial)

options.text: Combination NRT with 21 mg patch plus 4 mg nicotine gum for breakthrough cravings
options.rationale: Combination NRT (patch plus short-acting form) achieves quit rates approaching varenicline and is a reasonable alternative. However, this patient has failed NRT patches twice and may have developed a negative association with NRT. Varenicline offers a different mechanism of action (partial nicotinic agonist) and higher individual efficacy. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Addiction; RACGP Smoking Cessation Guidelines)

options.text: Nortriptyline 25 mg nocte titrated to 75 mg daily as a second-line cessation aid
options.rationale: Nortriptyline is a second-line option for smoking cessation, used when first-line agents are contraindicated or have failed. It carries anticholinergic side effects and requires ECG monitoring. Given that first-line options with superior evidence remain available, nortriptyline is premature at this stage. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Addiction; AMH)

explanation.summary: Varenicline is the most effective single-agent smoking cessation pharmacotherapy, acting as a partial agonist at alpha-4-beta-2 nicotinic acetylcholine receptors. It reduces both the reward from smoking and withdrawal symptoms. The EAGLES trial (8144 participants) resolved safety concerns by demonstrating no increased neuropsychiatric adverse events compared with NRT, bupropion, or placebo in patients with and without psychiatric illness. A standard 12-week course is recommended, with extension to 24 weeks for those at high relapse risk.
key_points: Varenicline has the highest single-agent efficacy for smoking cessation
EAGLES trial confirmed neuropsychiatric safety in patients with stable psychiatric illness
Standard titration over the first week reduces nausea (most common side effect)
PBS-subsidised in Australia for one course per 12 months
explanation.pearls: Varenicline achieves continuous abstinence rates of approximately 44% at weeks 9-12 versus 18% for placebo, and the EAGLES trial (2016) led to the removal of the FDA black box warning for neuropsychiatric events.

stem: A 21-year-old male university student is brought to the emergency department by police after being found agitated and disoriented in a public park. His friends report he smoked a large quantity of high-potency cannabis at a party 6 hours ago. He has no prior psychiatric history and no family history of psychotic illness. He believes people at the party poisoned him and that police are conspiring against him. He is visually distressed and pacing around the cubicle.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 108, BP 142/88, RR 18, SpO2 99% on room air, T 37.0
data_table.Examination: Agitated, paranoid, disorganised speech, persecutory delusions, no hallucinations elicited, conjunctival injection, dry mouth, pupils mildly dilated, oriented to person only, urine drug screen positive for THC
data_table.Investigations: FBC normal, UEC normal, LFTs normal, BSL 6.2 mmol/L, urine drug screen THC positive, alcohol level undetectable

lead_in: What is the most appropriate initial management approach?

options.text: Commence oral olanzapine 10 mg for psychosis and arrange psychiatric admission
options.rationale: While antipsychotic medication may be needed if agitation is severe and unresponsive to de-escalation, committing to a full antipsychotic treatment course and psychiatric admission is premature. Cannabis-induced psychosis typically resolves within hours to days of cessation. The priority is supportive management in a low-stimulus environment with benzodiazepines for agitation. (Source: RANZCP; Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic)

options.text: Administer IM haloperidol 10 mg and IM midazolam 10 mg for rapid tranquillisation
options.rationale: This patient is agitated but pacing and verbally communicable. Aggressive parenteral rapid tranquillisation at these high doses is disproportionate. Oral medications and de-escalation should be attempted first. IM haloperidol 10 mg is a high dose with risk of acute dystonia and oversedation. Graduated response is the recommended approach per Australian guidelines. (Source: NSW Health Acute Behavioural Disturbance Guidelines; RANZCP)

options.text: Discharge with safety-netting as cannabis psychosis is self-limiting and requires no treatment
options.rationale: While cannabis-induced psychosis is often self-limiting, this patient is currently acutely psychotic, paranoid, and disoriented. Discharge without adequate observation is unsafe. He requires monitoring in a low-stimulus environment until psychotic symptoms resolve and he is oriented and safe for discharge with appropriate follow-up. (Source: RANZCP; Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic)

options.text: Administer IV flumazenil to reverse the cannabis intoxication effects on GABA receptors
options.rationale: Flumazenil is a benzodiazepine antagonist with no activity at cannabinoid receptors. Cannabis acts primarily on CB1 receptors, not GABA receptors. There is no specific pharmacological antagonist for cannabis intoxication. Flumazenil administration is inappropriate and could precipitate seizures if unreported benzodiazepine co-ingestion is present. (Source: AMH; Toxicology Guidelines)

options.text: Place in a low-stimulus environment with verbal de-escalation and oral diazepam as needed
options.rationale: The initial management of cannabis-induced psychosis centres on supportive care in a low-stimulus environment with verbal de-escalation. Oral benzodiazepines (diazepam 5-10 mg) are first-line for agitation, providing anxiolysis and sedation without the extrapyramidal risks of antipsychotics. Observation until symptoms resolve (typically 6-24 hours for uncomplicated cannabis psychosis) with reassessment for ongoing psychotic symptoms is the standard approach. (Source: RANZCP; Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic)

explanation.summary: Cannabis-induced psychosis typically presents with paranoia, disorganisation, and anxiety within hours of use, particularly with high-potency products. Management is primarily supportive with a calm environment, de-escalation, and benzodiazepines for agitation. Symptoms usually resolve within hours to days. However, cannabis-induced psychosis is a significant risk factor for subsequent primary psychotic disorder, with approximately 20-30% of patients developing schizophrenia within 3 years. Follow-up is essential.
key_points: Supportive care in a low-stimulus environment is the initial priority
Benzodiazepines are first-line for cannabis-induced agitation
Symptoms typically resolve within 6-24 hours of cessation
20-30% of cannabis-induced psychosis cases progress to primary psychotic disorder
explanation.pearls: High-potency cannabis (THC >10% with low CBD content) carries approximately 5 times the psychosis risk of low-potency cannabis, and daily use of high-potency products accounts for a significant population-attributable fraction of first-episode psychosis presentations.

stem: A 36-year-old woman with a history of bipolar I disorder is brought to the emergency department by her partner. She has been increasingly withdrawn over the past 3 days and for the past 18 hours has been mute, refusing food and fluids, and staring blankly. Her bipolar disorder is managed with lithium 900 mg daily. She has no history of substance use. She was under the care of her community mental health team for a depressive episode over the past 4 weeks.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 96, BP 148/92, T 37.4, RR 16, SpO2 98% on room air
data_table.Examination: Mute, immobile in bed, eyes open and staring, waxy flexibility present on limb positioning (arms remain in passively placed positions for >30 seconds), negativism when attempts made to open her mouth, no response to verbal commands, maintains posture despite apparent discomfort
data_table.Investigations: Lithium level 0.8 mmol/L (therapeutic), FBC normal, UEC normal, CRP 8 mg/L, TFTs normal, urinalysis no infection

lead_in: What is the most appropriate initial diagnostic and therapeutic intervention?

options.text: Administer IV lorazepam 1-2 mg and observe for improvement in catatonic signs within 30 minutes
options.rationale: The lorazepam challenge is both diagnostic and therapeutic for catatonia. This patient displays classic catatonic features: mutism, immobility, waxy flexibility, negativism, and posturing. A positive response to IV lorazepam (improvement in at least one catatonic sign within 10-30 minutes) supports the diagnosis and guides ongoing treatment with scheduled benzodiazepines. The Bush-Francis Catatonia Rating Scale should be scored before and after the challenge. (Source: RANZCP; BMJ Best Practice: Catatonia)

options.text: Commence oral olanzapine 10 mg for presumed psychotic depression with catatonic features
options.rationale: Antipsychotics should be used with caution in catatonia as they may worsen symptoms and precipitate neuroleptic malignant syndrome (NMS), which shares features with malignant catatonia. Antipsychotics are not first-line treatment for catatonia. The initial intervention should be a benzodiazepine challenge. (Source: RANZCP; BMJ Best Practice: Catatonia)

options.text: Arrange urgent brain MRI to exclude organic causes before any pharmacological treatment
options.rationale: While organic causes of catatonia should be investigated, the clinical presentation in the context of known bipolar disorder with a recent depressive episode is consistent with psychiatric catatonia. Brain imaging is appropriate but should not delay the lorazepam challenge, which is both safe and diagnostically informative. Investigation and treatment proceed in parallel. (Source: RANZCP; BMJ Best Practice: Catatonia)

options.text: Increase lithium dose to achieve a higher therapeutic level for the depressive episode
options.rationale: The lithium level is therapeutic at 0.8 mmol/L. Increasing the dose would risk toxicity without addressing the catatonia. Catatonia is a distinct syndrome requiring specific treatment (benzodiazepines or ECT) and does not respond to mood stabiliser dose escalation alone. The depressive episode context is important but the catatonia takes treatment priority. (Source: AMH; RANZCP)

options.text: Commence IV antibiotics empirically for possible autoimmune encephalitis presenting as catatonia
options.rationale: While autoimmune encephalitis (particularly anti-NMDA receptor encephalitis) can present with catatonic features, the clinical context of known bipolar disorder with a recent depressive episode makes psychiatric catatonia more likely. Anti-NMDA receptor encephalitis would typically also include seizures, dysautonomia, and movement disorders. A lorazepam challenge is the appropriate first step. (Source: BMJ Best Practice: Catatonia)

explanation.summary: Catatonia is a psychomotor syndrome that can occur in the context of mood disorders, psychotic disorders, medical conditions, or autoimmune encephalitis. The key features include mutism, immobility, waxy flexibility, negativism, posturing, and stereotypy. The lorazepam challenge (1-2 mg IV with repeat scoring) is the gold standard diagnostic and initial therapeutic intervention. Benzodiazepines are first-line treatment, with ECT reserved for refractory cases. Antipsychotics should be avoided as they can worsen catatonia and precipitate NMS.
key_points: Lorazepam challenge is both diagnostic and therapeutic for catatonia
Benzodiazepines are first-line treatment; antipsychotics may worsen catatonia
Bipolar disorder is the most common psychiatric cause of catatonia
Malignant catatonia requires urgent ECT referral
explanation.pearls: The lorazepam challenge has a sensitivity of approximately 60-80% for catatonia; a negative challenge does not exclude the diagnosis, and patients who fail to respond to benzodiazepines should be referred for ECT, which has response rates exceeding 80% in catatonia.

subtopic_detail: Functional neurological disorder diagnosis and management
stem: A 28-year-old woman presents to the neurology clinic with a 3-week history of left-sided leg weakness causing difficulty walking. Symptoms began after a minor workplace injury. She has no history of neurological disease. MRI brain and spine are normal. Nerve conduction studies are normal. On examination, her left leg gives way inconsistently, and she has a positive Hoover sign. She appears genuinely distressed by her symptoms and is unable to work.

data_table.Examination: Inconsistent left leg weakness (gives way on direct testing but normal involuntary patterns during distraction), positive Hoover sign (hip extension weakness reverses with contralateral hip flexion), normal reflexes, downgoing plantars bilaterally, intact sensation, La belle indifference not present
data_table.Investigations: MRI brain and complete spine normal, nerve conduction studies normal, FBC/UEC/TFTs/B12 normal

lead_in: What is the most appropriate approach to communicating the diagnosis?

options.text: Explain the symptoms are stress-related and refer for psychological therapy alone
options.rationale: Framing FND as purely stress-related is reductive and may be perceived as dismissive. While psychological factors can contribute, FND is understood as a disorder of nervous system function, not simply a stress response. Management requires a multidisciplinary approach including physiotherapy, not psychology alone. (Source: BMJ Best Practice: FND; NICE)

options.text: Explain the positive diagnostic signs, name the condition, and plan multidisciplinary treatment
options.rationale: Best practice for FND diagnosis involves naming the condition (functional neurological disorder), explaining the positive clinical signs that support the diagnosis (e.g. Hoover sign demonstrates the nervous system has the capacity for normal function but is not sending signals correctly in the usual way), validating the patient's experience as real and not fabricated, and planning multidisciplinary care including specialist physiotherapy. This approach reduces diagnostic uncertainty and improves engagement. (Source: BMJ Best Practice: FND; NICE; Lancet Neurology FND consensus)

options.text: Reassure that all tests are normal and the weakness should resolve spontaneously over time
options.rationale: Dismissive reassurance based on normal investigations is unhelpful and may reinforce the patient's distress. FND is a positive diagnosis made on clinical signs, not a diagnosis of exclusion. Without active multidisciplinary treatment, symptoms may persist or worsen. Spontaneous resolution occurs in only a minority of cases with established symptoms. (Source: BMJ Best Practice: FND; Lancet Neurology FND consensus)

options.text: Request repeat MRI with contrast and electromyography before committing to a functional diagnosis
options.rationale: The diagnosis is supported by positive clinical signs (Hoover sign, inconsistency) with normal investigations. Repeating investigations delays treatment and may reinforce illness behaviour. FND is a rule-in diagnosis based on positive examination findings, not a rule-out diagnosis requiring exhaustive investigation. (Source: BMJ Best Practice: FND; NICE)

options.text: Confront the patient with the examination inconsistencies to demonstrate symptom fabrication
options.rationale: FND symptoms are not fabricated or under voluntary control. Confronting the patient with inconsistencies as evidence of fabrication is both clinically incorrect and therapeutically harmful. It damages the therapeutic alliance and may worsen symptoms. The inconsistency on examination is a positive diagnostic sign, not evidence of malingering. (Source: BMJ Best Practice: FND; Lancet Neurology FND consensus)

explanation.summary: Functional neurological disorder is diagnosed based on positive clinical signs demonstrating internal inconsistency of neurological function, such as the Hoover sign. It is not a diagnosis of exclusion. Communication of the diagnosis should name the condition, demonstrate the positive signs to the patient, validate their experience, and establish a treatment plan including specialist physiotherapy. Outcomes are improved by early diagnosis, clear communication, and multidisciplinary treatment.
key_points: FND is a positive diagnosis based on clinical signs, not a diagnosis of exclusion
Hoover sign and other inconsistency signs are rule-in diagnostic features
Symptoms are real and involuntary, not fabricated
Multidisciplinary treatment including specialist physiotherapy is the evidence-based approach
explanation.pearls: The Hoover sign has a sensitivity of approximately 63% and specificity of 100% for functional leg weakness; it demonstrates that involuntary hip extension (during contralateral flexion) is normal, confirming intact motor pathways that are not activated during voluntary testing.

stem: A 52-year-old woman with stable schizophrenia managed on clozapine 400 mg daily presents requesting support to quit smoking. She smokes 30 cigarettes per day. She previously tried varenicline but discontinued it after 2 weeks due to severe nausea and vomiting despite dose adjustment. She has no history of cardiovascular disease or seizures. Her clozapine level is 0.52 mg/L. She is motivated to attempt quitting again.

data_table.Examination: Stable mental state, no psychotic symptoms, BMI 32, PHQ-9 score 6
data_table.Investigations: Clozapine level 0.52 mg/L (target 0.35-0.60), FBC: WCC 5.8 x10^9/L, neutrophils 3.2 x10^9/L

lead_in: What is the most appropriate smoking cessation pharmacotherapy for this patient?

options.text: Retry varenicline at a lower maintenance dose of 0.5 mg twice daily long-term
options.rationale: The patient discontinued varenicline due to severe nausea and vomiting despite dose adjustment. While reduced-dose varenicline is an option, severe GI intolerance that led to discontinuation suggests an alternative approach is more appropriate. Forcing re-trial of a poorly tolerated medication reduces adherence. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Addiction; RACGP Smoking Cessation Guidelines)

options.text: Commence bupropion 150 mg twice daily as an alternative first-line cessation agent
options.rationale: Bupropion lowers the seizure threshold and may interact with clozapine, which also lowers seizure threshold. The combination increases the risk of seizures. Additionally, bupropion inhibits CYP2D6, which may affect clozapine metabolism. Combination NRT is a safer alternative in this context. (Source: AMH; Therapeutic Guidelines: Addiction)

options.text: Advise nicotine e-cigarette use as a harm reduction strategy without other pharmacotherapy
options.rationale: While e-cigarettes are used by some as a harm reduction tool, they are not a recommended first-line smoking cessation strategy in Australian clinical guidelines. Evidence-based pharmacotherapy should be offered first. The long-term safety profile of e-cigarettes remains uncertain, and TGA-approved cessation aids have stronger evidence. (Source: RACGP Smoking Cessation Guidelines; Therapeutic Guidelines: Addiction)

options.text: Commence nicotine patch 21 mg daily plus nicotine mouth spray for breakthrough cravings
options.rationale: Combination NRT (long-acting patch plus short-acting oral form) is an evidence-based approach with efficacy approaching that of varenicline. It avoids the seizure risk of bupropion in a clozapine-treated patient and the GI intolerance of varenicline. Importantly, smoking cessation reduces the induction of CYP1A2, which will increase clozapine levels - the clozapine dose must be reduced by approximately 30-50% and levels monitored closely after quitting. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Addiction; RACGP Smoking Cessation Guidelines; AMH)

options.text: Defer smoking cessation until clozapine is switched to a less metabolically affected antipsychotic
options.rationale: Smoking cessation should not be deferred due to clozapine treatment. The interaction between smoking cessation and clozapine metabolism is well-characterised and manageable with proactive dose reduction and level monitoring. The health benefits of smoking cessation far outweigh the inconvenience of clozapine dose adjustment. (Source: AMH; Therapeutic Guidelines: Addiction)

explanation.summary: Smoking cessation in patients on clozapine requires careful pharmacokinetic management. Cigarette smoke induces CYP1A2, which metabolises clozapine. Cessation reduces this induction, causing clozapine levels to rise by 50-70% if the dose is not adjusted. Combination NRT is preferred when varenicline is not tolerated and bupropion carries seizure risk. Clozapine dose should be reduced by approximately 30-50% at the quit date with close monitoring of levels and clinical state.
key_points: Combination NRT achieves efficacy approaching varenicline
Bupropion and clozapine both lower seizure threshold, making their combination risky
Smoking cessation increases clozapine levels by 50-70% via reduced CYP1A2 induction
Clozapine dose must be reduced proactively when a patient quits smoking
explanation.pearls: The CYP1A2 induction by cigarette smoke (polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons, not nicotine) means that NRT does not affect clozapine metabolism, but stopping smoking does - clozapine levels must be checked within 72 hours of the quit date and the dose typically reduced by 30-50% to avoid toxicity.

stem: A 30-year-old woman with bipolar I disorder has been stable on sodium valproate 1500 mg daily for 5 years. She presents at 6 weeks gestation having just discovered an unplanned pregnancy. Her pregnancy test was positive 3 days ago. She is enrolled in the pregnancy prevention program but her LARC (Implanon) was removed 2 months ago when she changed GPs and a replacement was not organised. She is distressed and wants to continue the pregnancy. She has had three severe manic episodes requiring hospitalisation before stabilisation on valproate.

data_table.Examination: Euthymic, anxious about pregnancy, no psychotic features, well-engaged
data_table.Investigations: Valproate level 92 mg/L, beta-hCG consistent with 6-week gestation, FBC and LFTs normal

lead_in: What is the most appropriate management plan?

options.text: Continue valproate at the current dose as the risk period for neural tube defects has passed
options.rationale: Neural tube closure occurs at approximately 28 days post-conception (6 weeks gestational age). While the critical period for neural tube defects may have passed, valproate exposure throughout pregnancy is associated with additional risks including cardiac malformations, cleft palate, hypospadias, and - most significantly - neurodevelopmental impairment affecting IQ and risk of autism spectrum disorder. Continued exposure must be weighed against relapse risk. (Source: ACSQHC Valproate Safety; AMH; RANZCP)

options.text: Immediately cease valproate without replacement and commence folic acid 5 mg daily
options.rationale: Abrupt cessation of valproate in a patient with a history of three severe manic episodes risks precipitating mania or psychosis during the critical first trimester. A manic relapse during pregnancy poses its own risks to mother and foetus. Any medication change must be gradual with concurrent introduction of a safer alternative. Folic acid should be commenced but is insufficient alone. (Source: RANZCP; COPE Guidelines; AMH)

options.text: Plan cross-taper from valproate to lithium with close psychiatric and obstetric monitoring
options.rationale: A collaborative decision involving the patient, psychiatrist, and obstetrician is needed. Cross-tapering from valproate to lithium is the recommended approach when valproate must be ceased in pregnancy. Lithium has a lower overall teratogenic risk (cardiac malformation rate approximately 1-2% vs valproate's 5-10% major malformation rate and 30-40% neurodevelopmental effects). The cross-taper should be gradual to minimise relapse risk. Folic acid 5 mg daily should be commenced. High-resolution ultrasound at 18-20 weeks is recommended. (Source: RANZCP; COPE Guidelines; AMH)

options.text: Reduce valproate to 500 mg daily as a dose-dependent risk reduction strategy throughout pregnancy
options.rationale: While valproate teratogenicity shows some dose-dependence (higher risk above 700-1000 mg daily), even low-dose valproate carries substantially higher risk than alternatives such as lithium. Dose reduction without switching still exposes the foetus to the ongoing neurodevelopmental risks of valproate. If the pregnancy is to continue, switching to a safer agent is the recommended approach. (Source: ACSQHC Valproate Safety; AMH; RANZCP)

options.text: Continue valproate and add lamotrigine as augmentation to allow later valproate cessation
options.rationale: Adding lamotrigine while continuing valproate delays the removal of the teratogenic agent. Valproate also inhibits the metabolism of lamotrigine, requiring the lamotrigine dose to be halved and complicating titration. The priority is to cease valproate exposure as quickly as is safely achievable, with direct transition to an alternative mood stabiliser. (Source: AMH; RANZCP)

explanation.summary: Valproate in pregnancy carries a 5-10% major malformation rate and a 30-40% risk of neurodevelopmental effects including reduced IQ and autism spectrum disorder. When pregnancy is discovered, the priority is a collaborative plan to transition to a safer mood stabiliser while minimising relapse risk. Lithium is the preferred alternative for bipolar I disorder. The cross-taper must be gradual, and close psychiatric monitoring is essential throughout. Specialist perinatal mental health involvement is recommended.
key_points: Valproate causes 5-10% major malformations and 30-40% neurodevelopmental effects
Cross-taper to lithium is the recommended strategy in bipolar I pregnancy
Abrupt cessation risks manic relapse which poses its own foetal and maternal risks
Specialist perinatal psychiatry and high-risk obstetric care should be involved
explanation.pearls: The NICE-MHRA valproate data show that valproate at doses above 1000 mg/day is associated with a major congenital malformation rate of approximately 10-11%, compared with 2-3% at doses below 700 mg/day, though neurodevelopmental risk persists at all doses.

subtopic_detail: TCA overdose with wide QRS specific management
stem: A 28-year-old man is brought to the emergency department after an intentional overdose of his mother's dothiepin. His housemate found him 90 minutes after ingestion. He was initially drowsy but has since had a 30-second generalised tonic-clonic seizure in the ambulance that self-terminated. He has received IV sodium bicarbonate 100 mmol on arrival. His post-bicarbonate ECG still shows a QRS duration of 148 ms with a prominent R wave in aVR. His pH is now 7.52. He then develops a broad complex tachycardia at a rate of 156 bpm with a BP of 72/44.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 156 (broad complex tachycardia), BP 72/44, RR 22, SpO2 96% on 10 L/min, GCS 8
data_table.Examination: Dilated pupils, dry skin, obtunded, broad complex tachycardia on monitor, no response to voice
data_table.Investigations: ABG: pH 7.52, pCO2 28 mmHg, HCO3 24 mmol/L, lactate 4.8 mmol/L, K+ 3.2 mmol/L, ECG: wide QRS 148 ms, R aVR 6 mm, broad complex tachycardia 156 bpm

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next intervention for the arrhythmia?

options.text: Administer IV amiodarone 300 mg over 20 minutes for the broad complex tachycardia
options.rationale: Amiodarone is a Class III antiarrhythmic that also has sodium channel blocking properties (Class IA effect). In TCA overdose, any agent that further blocks sodium channels will worsen QRS prolongation and may precipitate intractable VF. Amiodarone is specifically contraindicated in sodium channel blocker toxicity. (Source: Toxicology Guidelines; Austin Health Toxicology)

options.text: Perform synchronised DC cardioversion at 150 J for the haemodynamically unstable VT
options.rationale: While cardioversion is indicated for haemodynamically unstable VT from other causes, TCA-induced wide complex tachycardia is often resistant to electrical cardioversion because the arrhythmia mechanism is sodium channel blockade rather than a re-entrant circuit. Further sodium bicarbonate to overcome the channel blockade is the specific treatment. Cardioversion may be attempted if bicarbonate fails, but sodium loading is the priority. (Source: Toxicology Guidelines; ANZCOR)

options.text: Administer IV procainamide 15 mg/kg to convert the broad complex tachycardia
options.rationale: Procainamide is a Class IA sodium channel blocker that is absolutely contraindicated in TCA overdose. It will compound the existing sodium channel blockade, widen the QRS further, and likely precipitate cardiac arrest. Class IA, IC, and III antiarrhythmics are all contraindicated. (Source: Toxicology Guidelines; AMH)

options.text: Administer IV magnesium sulfate 2 g for the broad complex tachycardia and hypokalaemia
options.rationale: While magnesium may be appropriate for torsades de pointes, the primary arrhythmia mechanism in TCA toxicity is sodium channel blockade producing a wide complex tachycardia, not a magnesium-responsive rhythm. The hypokalaemia should be corrected concurrently, but the arrhythmia-specific treatment is further sodium loading. (Source: Toxicology Guidelines; Austin Health Toxicology)

options.text: Administer a further 100 mmol IV sodium bicarbonate bolus and consider 20% lipid emulsion
